IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical gap for a low-resource language by building a new, larger Vietnamese readability corpus and deriving a formula that classifies easy/normal/difficult texts with ~81% accuracy (paper claims “accuracy up to 81%” and per-class accuracies >76–85%). Prior Vietnamese studies used very small corpora (20 and 54 documents), so the new corpus and baseline formula materially improve available resources. However, the method relies on standard surface features (sentence/word length, percent ‘difficult’ words/syllables) and does not appear to change theoretical understanding of readability, so impact is useful and enabling for the Vietnamese NLP community but not transformative.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states main claims (new corpus and a formula achieving ~81% accuracy) but provides no technical detail here on corpus size, annotation agreement (Fleiss' kappa is mentioned but no value), how the formula was derived or validated (no training/test split, cross‑validation, baselines or statistical tests), nor the full experimental protocol. Mathematical content is limited to feature definitions (Eqs.8–14) with no derivation of the final formula or error analysis. The methodology therefore lacks key controls and quantitative evidence to support the accuracy/robustness claims, so the conclusions are insufficiently supported.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a recognizable structure (intro, methods, experiments, conclusion) and states its contributions (new corpus and formula) early, but the flow is disrupted by repeated and duplicated text (e.g. multiple copies of the “Most famous studies…” paragraph) and LaTeX/artifact fragments. Notation is partly defined (ASLW, AWLS, PDW/PDS) but is inconsistent and contains typos (Eq.13/14 use PDWi; later PDS1/PDW1 is referenced) which reduces clarity. Figures/tables are cited (Table 2, Table 3) but not shown here, and several sentences contain grammatical issues and awkward phrasing, making some sections hard to follow.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (Vietnamese text readability) is squarely within NLP and the paper contributes a new corpus and a readability formula using surface features (average sentence/word length, % difficult words/syllables) and reports ~81% accuracy, which will interest the NLP community. However, the methods are largely heuristic/linear and rely on simple frequency lists (Dinh and Hao) with limited evaluation detail and no comparison to modern baselines or ML approaches, so it falls short of the methodological and evaluation standards expected at top-tier NLP/ML venues. Overall this is a borderline fit: relevant but probably better suited to a more applied or regional venue unless substantially extended.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper’s main novelty is building a new, larger Vietnamese readability corpus and producing a fitted linear readability formula for Vietnamese (claims in abstract and conclusion). However, the features and modeling approach are standard (average sentence/word lengths, percentage of difficult words/syllables using frequency lists; Sections 3.2 and Equations 8–14), and the formula appears to be a straightforward adaptation of classical readability indices rather than a fundamentally new method. The authors do distinguish their work from prior Vietnamese studies (noting earlier small corpora) but the technical contribution is largely incremental and language-specific rather than a non-trivial methodological advance.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper cites classic readability formulas (Dale–Chall, Flesch, Flesch–Kincaid, Gunning Fog, SMOG) and prior Vietnamese work (Nguyen & Henkin; Dinh and Hao 2015), but it does not present quantitative comparisons of the new formula against these baselines. Results (e.g. 76–85% per class, overall 81%) are reported only for the authors' new corpus and formula, with no side-by-side evaluation using the same data and metrics for prior methods. The paper discusses that Dinh & Hao’s frequency lists have low correlation with readability, but it neither evaluates prior Vietnamese formulas on the new corpus nor acknowledges cases where prior methods might match or outperform the proposal. Overall, comparisons are inadequate and key baseline evaluations are missing.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper reports feature definitions and summary results (e.g. Table 2 identifying word/sentence length and difficult-word percentages as important, Table 3 reporting ~81% overall accuracy) and notes corpus reliability via Fleiss’ kappa, but experimental details are sparse. There is only limited analysis of alternative frequency lists (PDS1/PDW1 vs corpus lists) but no systematic ablation or sensitivity study of the final formula, no dataset size/split or baseline comparisons, and only brief discussion of limitations and future work. These omissions make the evaluation shallow and hinder reproducibility.
